title: Add PodName to ContainerConfig to be exposed in inspect
body:
closes #15374 
<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *boaz0* **Once this PR has been reviewed and has the lgtm label**, please assign tomsweeneyredhat for approval by writing `/assign @tomsweeneyredhat` in a comment. For more information see:[The Kubernetes Code Review Process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process). The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Please add a test to inspect for it,
@vrothberg I probably misunderstood. I thought `PodName` was supposed to be added to the container metadata.

> I'd expected a PR that adds the Pod ID as a label for the compat endpoint. @mheon WDYT?

This sounds problematic. Labels should only be set by external users/tools.
Docker-compose and ansible and possible more tools would think that the container was changed and will try to recreate it because the labels no longer match their expected ones.

> @Luap99 @vrothberg what about adding `PodName` to annotations?

I do not object adding that to annotations but I think we need to agree on what problem we want to solve.

How about displaying annotations in `/containers/{name}/json`? This information is shown when we do `inspect <container-name>`. 
It will be available both on `compat` and `podman inspect` and won't interfere `Ansible`/`DockerCompose`/etc.

**EDIT**: OK I see that Docker doesn't show annotations in its API... that's a problem.
@vrothberg
Yes, the Docker API does not know annotations.  I think there's nothing we can do.  I am not against adding a the PodName but it does not really address the initial problem.
Why do we care about the Docker-compat endpoint, again? Is there a reason the Desktop folks aren't using the Libpod endpoints, which expose a lot more of the Podman-specific bits?
@mheon @boaz0 @vrothberg what should we do with this PR?
I think we can close it.  The original issue isn't needed anymore.
